content:
𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝘂𝗽𝗿𝗲𝗺𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝘂𝗿𝘁 𝗵𝗮𝘀 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗳𝗳𝗶𝗿𝗺𝗲𝗱 𝗮𝗻 𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗮𝗻𝘁 𝗽𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝗶𝗻 𝘀𝘂𝗶𝘁𝘀 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗰𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗰 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲.
Merely filing a suit within the limitation period is not enough. A plaintiff must continuously prove both readiness (financial capacity) and willingness (conduct showing intent to perform the contract). Delayed action, absence of proof of funds at the relevant time, or passive conduct can disentitle a party from obtaining the equitable relief of specific performance.
In Mohammed Khaleel (D) Through LRs. v. Jayamma (2026), the Court dismissed the appeal, holding that readiness and willingness must exist throughout the contractual period and that unexplained delay, even within limitation, can be fatal to the claim.

 In specific performance suits, equity favors the vigilant, not those who merely act within limitation.
